q: The burning wood in the air releases ___ and light energies.
The fireworks display illustrates a spectacular release of energy ($light$ and $heat$) in a ___ reaction.
___ is the chemical reaction that occurs when plants use energy from the sun, carbon dioxide, and water to synthesise the food that the plant needs to survive.
When we burn ___ for energy, chemical changes take place during the reaction and release huge amounts of energy.
Except for the energy produced from nuclear reactors, all of man's energy resources are chemicals or involve the ___ of energy through chemical reactions.
Energy changes are central to chemical reactions and understanding the role of energy in ___ provides for our daily life.
Whenever a chemical reaction (chemical change) occurs, ___ is usually transferred to or from the surroundings.
The energy change (gain or loss) during the reaction can be ___.
Changing ___ of matter (physical change) also involve energy changes.
___ (e.g., boiling water), melting (e.g., melting ice), condensation... are some examples of changing states around us in everyday life.
Mothball and dry ice ___ without melting.
There are various kinds of energy such as ___ energy (due to the movement).
___ energy is due to the position.
Energy can neither be created nor destroyed as stated in the ___.
Energy can change from one form to another without a net loss or gain of energy and it is called ___.
Electric motor converts electrical energy into ___ energy.
Hair dryer converts electrical energy into ___ energy.
___ converts chemical energy into electrical energy.
Automobile engine converts chemical energy into ___ energy.
Solar cell converts light energy into ___ energy.
All chemical reactions involve ___ changes.
___ is the energy that is stored in the bonds of chemical compounds such as atoms and molecules.
Humans release chemical energy during ___ and use it to power systems inside the body.
Plants store chemical energy in ___ formed during photosynthesis.
Whenever a chemical reaction takes place between reactants to form products, there is always an energy change usually in the form of ___.
Chemical reactions often involve changes in energy due to the ___ and formation of bonds.
The energy change is due to the difference in the amounts of ___ between the reactant and the product particles in a chemical reaction.
The interactions between particles can produce ___ or heating effects that are used in everyday applications.
A chemical reaction involves the breaking of bonds in the ___ and the forming of bonds in the products.
Energy is absorbed to break bonds, and energy is ___ when bonds are formed during the process of dissolving.
Chemical reactions provide ___ which is the result of interactions between ions in an electrolyte.

Some reactions take place on their own under a given set of conditions, and some are needed to provide energy for the reaction to proceed. A process that can take place on its own accord is called ___.
___ processes need the energy to take place.
Adding a solution of dilute sulphuric acid to a solution of ___ is an example of a spontaneous reaction.
The reaction between magnesium and oxygen, and the reaction between copper and oxygen are both ___ reactions.
___ is exothermic and spontaneous.
When the ice melts at room temperature, that is a spontaneous and ___ process.
When sulphuric acid is slowly poured into water, the flask or beaker becomes hot. This is heat being released to the ___ by the reacting system.
Heat being released to the surroundings by the reacting system is known as an ___ reaction.
Examples of exothermic reactions are burning candle, the combustion of a fuel, the neutralisation reaction, respiration, and ___.
Exothermic reactions give out energy. So, there is a ___ rise.
In exothermic reactions, the energy of the product is ___ than the energy of the reactants because energy has been released during the reaction.
When ammonium chloride is dissolved in water, the flask becomes quite ___ to the touch.
Heat being absorbed from the surroundings by the reacting system is known as an ___ reaction.
Examples of endothermic reactions are melting of ice, cooking an egg, evaporating liquid water, and ___ of limestone.
Endothermic reactions take in energy from the surroundings. So, there is a ___ fall.
In endothermic reactions, the energy of the products is ___ than the energy of the reactants because energy has been absorbed during the reaction.
Energy changes in chemical reactions are caused by the ___ and breaking of chemical bonds.
Bonds in the reactants must be ___ first.
Bond breaking is an ___ process.
Energy is ___ when new bonds form.
Bond making is an ___ process.
In an exothermic reaction: total energy absorbed for bond breaking $<$ ___.
c: total energy released for bond making

q: In an endothermic reaction: total energy absorbed for bond breaking $>$ ___.
c: total energy released for bond making

q: ___ is the specific part of the universe that contains the reaction or process which is studied.
___ are everything in the universe other than the system.
The total chemical energy of a substance is called its ___ (or heat content).
___ is the symbol for enthalpy.
The change in enthalpy describes the amount of heat transferred at ___ during a chemical reaction.
Heat changes can be measured by using a ___.
The heat absorbed or released in a process occurring at constant pressure is called the ___.
Enthalpy change is given the symbol ___.
The unit of enthalpy change is ___ ($kJ\ mol^{-1}$).
Enthalpy is not measured directly, however, the change in enthalpy is measured, which is the heat ___ by the system.
For an endothermic reaction where energy is absorbed from the surroundings, the total energy of the products is ___ than that of the reactants.
For an exothermic reaction where energy is released to the surroundings, the total energy of the products is ___ than that of the reactants.
The heat absorbed or released in a process occurring at standard conditions of temperature and pressure is called the ___, $\Delta H^\theta$.
The standard temperature chosen for energy changes is $25\ ^{\circ}C$ = ___ $K$.
The standard pressure chosen for energy changes is $1\ atm$ = $760\ mmHg$ = ___ $kPa$.
A ___ is a balanced chemical equation that includes the physical states of all reactants and products and the standard enthalpy change ($\Delta H^\theta$ is negative or positive).
It is usual to indicate the physical state of each substance by the appropriate letter places in brackets after the formula; ___ for solid.
It is usual to indicate the physical state of each substance by the appropriate letter places in brackets after the formula; ___ for liquid.
The ___ of a reaction ($\Delta H_r^\theta$) is the enthalpy change when the molar quantities of reactants stated in the chemical equation react to give products under standard conditions.
The ___ of a compound is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its elements under standard conditions.
The symbol for standard enthalpy change of formation is ___.
Note that the state symbol for carbon is shown as "___" because it is the most stable form.
The ___ is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance (element or compound) is burnt completely in oxygen under standard conditions.
The symbol for standard enthalpy change of combustion is ___.
Combustion reactions are always ___ and the enthalpy changes are always negative.
The ___ is the enthalpy change when solutions of an acid and an alkali react together under standard conditions to produce one mole of water.
The symbol for standard enthalpy change of neutralisation is ___.
The neutralisation reaction is exothermic and the enthalpy change is ___.
Experimentally, $\Delta H$ is not measured. The heat change, $q$, is measured by the equation: ___.
In the equation $q = mc\Delta T$, $m$ is the ___ of the reactants.
In the equation $q = mc\Delta T$, $c$ is the ___ of materials.
A ___ is a device to measure the amount of heat released or absorbed in reactions that occur in solutions.
The ___ of a substance, $\Delta H_{sol}$, is the enthalpy change when one mole of the substance is dissolved in water.
Density of water is ___ $g\ cm^{-3}$.
The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of one gram of water by one degree is known as the ___ of water and is given the symbol $c$.
The specific heat capacity of water has a value of ___ $J\ g^{-1}\ ^{\circ}C^{-1}$.
A ___ is a type of calorimeter for determining the heat of combustion by igniting a sample in a high pressure of oxygen in a sealed vessel and measuring the resulting temperature rise.

There are very few reactions whose enthalpy change can be measured directly by measuring the change in temperature in a calorimeter. In some cases, the reaction is difficult, even impossible, to carry out individually. By using ___, the enthalpy change for such reactions can be determined indirectly.
___ states that the enthalpy change for a chemical reaction is the same, whatever route is taken from reactants to products.
Hess's law can be illustrated by drawing an ___.
Note that the standard enthalpy change for the formation of an element in its ___ is zero and no need to take it into account.
To determine the enthalpy change of reaction from enthalpy changes of formation, the elements are written at the bottom with arrows pointing ___.
To determine the enthalpy change of formation from enthalpy changes of combustion, the corresponding ___ should be considered.
The combustion products (usually ___ and water) are written at the bottom.
To determine the enthalpy change of formation from enthalpy changes of combustion, draw the ___ arrows that go from elements and compounds to combustion products.
Enthalpy changes are due to the breaking and forming of bonds. Breaking a bond ___ energy.
Breaking a bond absorbs energy ($\Delta H$ is ___).
Making a bond ___ energy.
Making a bond releases energy ($\Delta H$ is ___).
The energy needed to make or break bonds is called ___ (bond enthalpy).
The higher the bond enthalpy, the more energy needed to break the bond, and the ___ is the bond.
The symbol for bond enthalpy is ___.
___ refers to the bond enthalpy of a mole of single bonds between carbon and hydrogen atoms.
When new bonds are formed the amount of energy released is the ___ as the amount of energy absorbed when the same type of bond is broken.
___ can also be used to find approximate values of enthalpy change of a reaction using bond enthalpies.
The ___ can be used to calculate the enthalpy change of a reaction that cannot be measured directly.
In burning fuels (such as paraffin, coal, propane, and butane) the ___ reaction takes place during the reaction.
Hand warmers produce heat from the ___ reaction.
The endothermic reaction takes place during cooking food, baking bread, boiling water, and ___ the food.
